coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Kong, Depeng
Fu, Yang
Ye, Qianghua
Wang, Yinyin
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: Herein we designed a simple and effective method for synthesizing carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide nanocomposite with high photocatalytic activity. The as-prepared carbon nanodots/ tungsten trioxide has strong photoabsorption under visible light irradiation. Then, carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide was successfully applied to the degradation of methylene blue. The photodegradation efficiency of methylene blue can be reached as high as 100% after 0.5h visible light illumination. In addition, carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide could also be used to degrade rhodamine B and methyl orange. Most importantly, the photocatalytic activity of carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide did not exhibit obvious changes after five cycles. The results indicate that carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide has potential applications in the degradation of organic pollutants in industrial waste water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide
Photocatalyst
Photocatalytic activity
Stability
Degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CNDs (a) (inset: the particles size distribution histograms of CNDs). FTIR spectrum of CNDs (b). XPS survey scan of CNDs (c). The enlarged regions for C1s, N1s, O1s of CNDs (d–f).
Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs (a). Fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength from 300 to 370nm in 10nm increments) of CNDs (b).
TEM image (a), FTIR (b), XPS survey scan (c) and XRD patterns (d) of CNDs/WO3.
Degradation of MB by CNDs, WO3 and CNDs/WO3 under visible light (a) and in dark field (b).
Schematic illustration of the visible light-induced photodegradation of MB with CNDs/WO3 nanocomposite.

Repeated cycles up to 5 times illustrating the degradation of MB after 1.5h over the CNDs/WO3 nanocomposite under visible light irradiation. Reaction conditions: [MB]=10ug/mL, V=50mL, [catalyst]=30mg, visible lamp intensity=50mWcm−2.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we designed a simple and effective method for synthesizing carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide nanocomposite with high photocatalytic activity. The as-prepared carbon nanodots/ tungsten trioxide has strong photoabsorption under visible light irradiation. Then, carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide was successfully applied to the degradation of methylene blue. The photodegradation efficiency of methylene blue can be reached as high as 100% after 0.5h visible light illumination. In addition, carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide could also be used to degrade rhodamine B and methyl orange. Most importantly, the photocatalytic activity of carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide did not exhibit obvious changes after five cycles. The results indicate that carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide has potential applications in the degradation of organic pollutants in industrial waste water.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysts have attracted considerable attention because of their applications in degradation of pollutants and water purification [1–3]. The most representative photocatalyst titanium dioxide (TiO2) has excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, and high photocatalytic activity [4–6]. But TiO2 can only be excited by ultra violet light which limits its application [7]. In the field of visible-light photocatalysis, many efforts such as doping, co-catalyst loading have been made [8–11]. Therefore, it is significant to develop novel visible-light-driven photocatalysts to increase the utilization efficiency of solar light.

Photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been used as promising candidates for fluorescent materials in term of biology, environmental applications and energy conversion [12–16]. Recently, some reports have proved that surface-modified carbon nanoparticles can absorb visible light to improve photocatalytic activity. Especially, photocatalysts doped by CNDs show much better catalytic activity [17–21]. Thus, it is of great significance to investigate the particular photocatalytic application of CNDs.

Tungsten trioxide (WO3) is a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst due to its nontoxicity and resilience to photocorrosion [22,23]. While its rapid recombination of photo-generated charge carriers limits the photocatalytic activity. Thus, many attempts have been made to enhance the photocatalytic activity of WO3 [24–27]. Herein, WO3 was doped with CNDs through a simple hydrothermal method to enhance the photocatalytic activity. The result nanocomposite show high photocatalytic property and stability. It can be used for degrading organic pollutants such as methylene blue, rhodamine B and methyl orange under visible light irradiation.

Sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), hydrochloric acid, citric acid, diethylenetriamine, toluene, ethyl alcohol absolute, methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (Rh B) and methyl orange (MO) were purchased from the local company (http://www.tjhxsj.cn/). Quinine sulfate were supplied by Aladdin. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used in all aqueous solution preparation and washing.

CNDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and diethylenetriamine in deionized (DI) water. Briefly, 0.42g citric acid and 0.683mL diethylenetriamine was dispersed into 10mL DI water under magnetic stirring. Then the mixture was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200°C for 5h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was first neutralized with a Na2CO3 solution. The solid was obtained by rotary evaporation. Then the solid was dissolved in ethanol and the insoluble precipitate in the solution was removed by centrifugation. The solution was further purified through loading into dialysis bags (Mw=3000) for dialysis against distilled water for 4days. Eventually, pure CNDs were obtained by rotary evaporation.

CNDs/WO3 was synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of Na2WO4·2H2O and CNDs. A certain amount of CNDs were dissolved 20mL water with half an hour ultrasonication. Then Na2WO4·2H2O (3.3g) dissolved in the above dispersion and kept stirring 1h. The pH value of the above solution was adjusted to about 2 using 3M HCl solution. After half an hour stirring, the suspension was transferred to a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 180°C and maintained for 24h in an oven. After naturally cooled down to room temperature, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation. Then the result precipitate was washed with DI water and ethyl alcohol absolute several times, respectively. Subsequently, the product was dried under vacuum at 80°C about 6h. The obtained powder was then calcined at 300°C for 2h in air to obtain the CNDs/WO3 nanocomposite. Pure WO3 sample was also obtained through a similar procedure only in the absence of CNDs.

The photocatalytic activities of CNDs/WO3, pure WO3 and CNDs were evaluated by photodegradation of MB. A 150W high-pressure mercury lamp with a cut-off glass filter transmitting λ >400nm placed 10cm away from the reaction vessel was used as the light source. The illumination intensity was 50mWcm−2. The selected photocatalyst (30mg) was dispersed in 50mL MB aqueous solution (10μgmL−1). After stirred in the dark for 0.5h, the solution obtained a good dispersion and reached adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the organic molecules and the catalyst surface. At given intervals of illumination, the samples (3mL) of the reaction solution were taken out and centrifuged. Finally, the decrease in the concentration of dyes in filtrates were analyzed by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at λ =664nm. The degradation of Rh B and MO were under the same conditions. As control experiments, the degradation of MB, Rh B and MO were also investigated without the light illumination.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (TENSOR37) using KBr pallets. Wide-angle (10–808, 40kV/100mA) X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on a Rigaku D/max 2550 VB/PC diffractometer using nickel-filtered Cu Ka radiation with the wavelength of λ =1.5406Å. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on CNDs/WO3 nanocomposite which were drop-cast onto a carbon-coated Cu grid on an electronic microscope (HITACHI, H-7650). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were obtained using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary 500). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer with the excitation wavelength of 340nm. Particle sizes were measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The degradation of MB, Rh B and MO in water were measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901).

TEM was applied to investigate the morphologies of the CNDs. Fig.1 a shows that CDs are monodisperse with near spherical morphologies. Based on the statistical analysis of particles, the average size is about 3.9±0.5nm. The size distribution of CDs was calculated by measuring approximately 100 particles from the TEM image of CDs. FTIR was used to study the surface functional groups of CNDs (Fig.1b). The peaks centered at 3433cm−1 and 3140cm−1 come from the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, revealing a large quantity of hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of CNDs. The other two peaks around 1577cm−1 and 1400cm−1 are originated from CO and CN, respectively. The FTIR results indicate that the CNDs contain a lot of oxygen containing groups, which help CNDs to be well dispersed in water. XPS was used to analyze the composition and surface elements of CNDs (Fig.1c). The XPS survey spectrum of the CNDs shows three main peaks at 285, 400 and 530eV, which are ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The high resolution C1s reveals three peaks center at 285, 286, 288eV, attributing to the binding energy of CC, CN and CO (Fig.1d). This indicates the existence of carbon atoms connecting to N and O heteroatoms. The N1s spectrum shows two peaks around 400eV, which are assigned to the CNC and NH bands, respectively (Fig.1e). The spectrum of O1s was split into three peaks coming from the binding energy of CO, COH and COC (Fig.1f) [28,29]. The quantitative elemental determination by XPS shows that the main element C, O, N has a percentage of 64.28, 12.50, 0.71, respectively. These analyses suggest that the prepared nanoparticles are still carbon material.

The optical properties of CNDs were studied through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig.2a, CNDs showed strong absorbance around 210nm, which was ascribed to the π–π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains [30]. The other peak around 330nm indicated the n–π∗ transition of CO [31]. In order to understand the exact optical properties of CDs, fluorescence spectra were recorded under different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence spectra are generally broad and dependent on excitation wavelength, which can be explained by an “edge red-shift effect” [32]. As shown in Fig.2b, the fluorescence peaks shifted to longer wavelengths when the excitation wavelength changed from 300 to 370nm. The strongest emission peak was located at 442nm with the excitation wavelength at 340nm which consistent with previous fluorescence analyses. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was about 91%, using quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1mol/L H2SO4, λ ex =371nm) as a reference material. These results suggest that CNDs may be used as a powerful energy-transfer component in photocatalyst design for applications in environmental and energy issues.

Fig.3 a shows the TEM image of the nanocomposite CND/WO3. It is clearly seen that the CND/WO3 particles are uniformly distributed and CNDs have successfully doped with WO3. It can also be clearly seen form the FTIR spectrum of CND/WO3 (Fig.3b) that the OWO stretching vibration of WO3 crystal clearly appears at 815cm−1 as a broad absorption band [33]. In the XPS survey scan of CNDs/WO3, the new emerging peak of W 4f serves as an evidence for the formation of CNDs/WO3 (Fig.3c). The binding energy peaks located at 37.9eV are attributed to the spin–orbit splitting of the W 4f components, which is consistent with WO (Fig. S1). Similarly to the XPS survey of CNDs, CNDs/WO3 also has other three main peaks around 285, 400 and 530eV, which are ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. S1). Furthermore, XRD was used to demonstrate the formation of CND/WO3. The characteristic diffraction peaks of WO3 are corresponding well to typical Wurtzite WO3 phase (PDF No. 36-1451), suggesting monoclinic-type WO3 crystals were synthesized (Fig.3d). The main characteristic diffraction peaks of CND/WO3 are well consistent with those of the pristine WO3. Compared to WO3, a new diffraction peak centered at 31.13° attributing to the peak of CNDs appears for CNDs/WO3. It is presumably owing to that CNDs have highly doped with WO3.

The photocatalytic activity of the catalysts was tested for the degradation of MB, a kind of waste water pollutants. The degradation of MB under visible light and in dark filed were monitored by recording the decrease of the absorption (λ max =664nm). As shown in Fig.4 a, the degradation of MB decreases weakly with the time increasing in the presence of CNDs. While, only 35% of MB was degraded after 1.5h visible light irradiation. As known, WO3 is a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst. But the degradation velocity is also not satisfactory, only 78% of MB was degraded after 1.5h visible light irradiation. Particularly, a much higher photocatalytic activity was achieved when doping CNDs with WO3. The photodegradation efficiency of MB by CNDs/WO3 was 100% after 0.5h visible light irradiation. These results clearly demonstrate that doping CNDs with WO3 can greatly enhance their photocatalytic activity. In the control experiments, the degradation of MB by CNDs, WO3 and CNDs/WO3 in dark field were also investigated. Fig.4b shows that only trace degradation of MB can be observed without the light irradiation over CNDs, WO3 and CNDs/WO3. The results reveal that the degradation is photoinduced. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of CNDs/WO3 on Rh B and MO degradation were also studied. The results showed in Fig. S2 also indicate that CNDs/WO3 exhibits much higher activity than CNDs and WO3. Thus, the prepared CNDs/WO3 can work as effective photocatalysts for toxic organic pollutants degradation.

On the basis of the above results, it can be concluded that CNDs must play critical roles for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CNDs/WO3. Under visible light irradiation, CNDs/WO3 obtained high energy. The electrons (e−) in the valence band of WO3 were excited into the conduction band and followed by moving freely to the surface of CNDs through the photoinduced charge transfer process. Meanwhile, the holes (h+) leave from the valence band of WO3. Subsequently, h+ migrated to the surface of WO3 to produce OH
radicals from the surface H2O. e− could react with the dissolved O2 on the surface of CNDs to form
O2 − radicals. Which cause the degradation of MB. Then
O2 − could also produce OH
radicals which are also powerful oxidizing agents to degrade MB [20,34,35]. The schematic illustration of the mechanism for the photocatalysis of CNDs/WO3 is summarized in Fig. 5 . This phenomenon also shows that CNDs indeed play an important role in the higher degradation efficiency of MB. The degradation of Rh B and MO are in the similar photocatalysis process.

To evaluate the stability and reusability of the CNDs/WO3 nanocomposite photocatalysts, we repeated the photodegradation of MB with CNDs/WO3 under the same condition for five times. Fig. 6 shows that the photocatalytic degradation of MB by CNDs/WO3 under visible light irradiation with five times cycling uses are not evidently changed, and the yield is still 93.5% after repeating five times. Meanwhile, CNDs/WO3 can be easily recovered by centrifugation or sedimentation. Thus CNDs/WO3 has potential applications to eliminate the organic pollutants from waste water.

In this work, novel photocatalyst carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide was successfully synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method. Compared with carbon nanodots and tungsten trioxide, carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide exhibits much higher photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. The carbon nanodots in the nanocomposite can effectively trap electrons, thus hindering the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes and enhancing the photocatalytic property. Furthermore, the carbon nanodots/tungsten trioxide photocatalyst was found to be stable and reusable. The presented approach indicates that carbon nanomaterials may become a promising photocatalyst through rational design and provides an effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic activity and promote their practical applications in environmental water purification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21174103, 21374078) and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (No. 15JCYBJC18100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2015.12.043.
